meta.title: SF-SPSNA-3: Provider Setup, Consumer Security, and Release Operations

questions.stem: Which TWO actions should a provider take to request external network access from a native app? (Select TWO)

options.text: Define an EXTERNAL ACCESS INTEGRATION specifying the allowed network rules (hostnames and ports)
Declare the required external access integration in the app's manifest.yml
Embed API credentials in the listing description for consumer reference
Open unrestricted internet access by default in the application package

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because an EXTERNAL ACCESS INTEGRATION defines exactly which external hosts and ports the app is allowed to contact, making outbound network access explicit and auditable.

**B** is correct because the manifest.yml must declare the required external access integration so that consumers are informed of and can approve the external connectivity during installation.

**C** is incorrect because embedding credentials in listing descriptions is a severe security anti-pattern.

**D** is incorrect because unrestricted internet access is never available by default in native apps.

[Use app specifications to request controlled access](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/requesting-app-specs)

questions.stem: Which TWO security practices are recommended when developing native apps? (Select TWO)

options.text: Request the minimum necessary permissions in the manifest to maintain consumer trust
Define fine-grained application roles rather than a single all-access role
Request ACCOUNTADMIN-equivalent permissions to ensure all app features work without errors
Disable Snowflake's automatic audit logging within the app to reduce noise

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because requesting only necessary permissions reduces consumer concern about installing the app and limits the potential damage if the app or provider were ever compromised.

**B** is correct because fine-grained application roles (e.g., separate VIEWER, ANALYST, and ADMIN roles) allow consumers to grant each user only what they need, implementing least-privilege at the feature level.

**C** is incorrect because requesting ACCOUNTADMIN-level permissions is a security anti-pattern that will cause Marketplace review rejection and deter consumers.

**D** is incorrect because Snowflake's audit logging cannot be disabled by a native app, and disabling audit logs would violate consumer security requirements.

[Security Best Practices for Native Apps](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/native-apps-about)

questions.stem: Why are application roles a key security feature in native apps?

options.text: They let consumers grant only the specific privileges needed for each app feature to their users
They bypass Snowflake's RBAC model for app users
They force the provider to own all consumer data accessed by the app
They replace references as the consumer object access mechanism

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because application roles expose least-privilege access tiers within the app. A consumer can grant a user the VIEWER role (read-only features) or the ADMIN role (management features) rather than giving all users access to all app capabilities.

**B** is incorrect because application roles work within Snowflake's RBAC model, not outside it.

**C** is incorrect because the native app model specifically prevents providers from owning or accessing consumer data directly.

**D** is incorrect because application roles and references serve different purposes: references control object access, while roles control feature access.

[Configure the privileges required by an app](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/requesting-auto-privs)

questions.stem: Which provider-side object holds versioned artifacts and metadata for a native app?

options.text: Reader account
Masking policy
Application package
Warehouse cluster

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because reader accounts are for sharing data with consumers who do not have Snowflake accounts, not for storing native app artifacts.

**B** is incorrect because masking policies are used for column-level data security, not artifact storage.

**C** is correct because the application package is the provider-side container that stores versioned code artifacts, stages, and metadata before the app is released to consumers.

**D** is incorrect because warehouse clusters are compute resources, not artifact repositories.

[Application Package](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/creating-app-package)

questions.stem: Which statement best describes the native app trust model between providers and consumers?

options.text: Providers automatically receive all privileges in the consumer account upon installation
Consumers cannot inspect which permissions an app is requesting
Providers should request only the minimum consumer permissions necessary for each app feature
Application roles are optional and can be omitted for simpler apps

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because providers receive no automatic privileges in the consumer account; all access is explicitly requested and consumer-approved.

**B** is incorrect because consumers can review all requested permissions before granting them.

**C** is correct because the trust model is built on least privilege: providers should only request permissions strictly necessary for the features they offer, making it easier for consumers to trust and install the app.

**D** is incorrect because application roles are the fundamental mechanism for least-privilege feature access within the app.

[Native App Security Model](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/native-apps-about)

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about secrets management in native apps are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: Snowflake SECRET objects can store credentials that the app uses for external API calls
Secrets used by the app should be declared in the manifest so consumers can approve their use
API credentials should be stored as plain text in the app's setup.sql for easy access
Secrets can be written to the app's Streamlit session state so users can view them if needed

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Snowflake SECRET objects securely store credentials (API keys, OAuth tokens) that can be accessed by stored procedures and UDFs during execution without exposing the values in code.

**B** is correct because secrets that the app needs to use must be declared in the manifest so consumers are aware of and can approve the credential usage before installation.

**C** is incorrect because storing credentials in plain text in setup.sql means they are visible in the app's code artifact and pose a severe security risk.

**D** is incorrect because credentials should never be exposed to end users through session state or UI elements.

[Use app specifications to request controlled access](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/requesting-app-specs)

questions.stem: Which TWO approaches can be used to test Snowpark stored procedure logic in a native app? (Select TWO)

options.text: Install the app in a consumer-style test account and call the procedures via CALL statements
Bypass Snowflake security controls in the test environment to simplify testing
Use the Marketplace sandbox to run untested procedure code in production consumer accounts
Unit test the Python handler functions locally using pytest before packaging

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Python handler functions contain pure Python logic that can be extracted and unit-tested locally with pytest, providing fast feedback before any Snowflake deployment.

**B** is correct because integration testing in a consumer-style test account validates the full end-to-end flow including installation, privilege grants, procedure execution, and expected output.

**C** is incorrect because bypassing security controls in test environments can mask real bugs that will surface in production consumer accounts.

**D** is incorrect because running untested code in production consumer accounts risks corrupting consumer data.

[Install and test an app locally](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/installing-testing-application)

questions.stem: Which TWO mechanisms allow a native app to access consumer-owned objects safely? (Select TWO)

options.text: References that consumers explicitly grant to the app
Application roles that consumers assign to their users for app features
Masking policies automatically disabled by the app at install time
The provider inheriting SYSADMIN in the consumer account

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because references are the approved mechanism for a native app to request access to specific consumer-owned objects, with the consumer approving each reference.

**B** is correct because application roles define which features and objects within the app a consumer user can access, enabling least-privilege access control.

**C** is incorrect because native apps cannot disable consumer masking policies; consumer governance controls are preserved.

**D** is incorrect because providers receive no account roles in the consumer account through app installation.

[Request references and object-level privileges from consumers](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/requesting-refs)

questions.stem: Which TWO are valid strategies for debugging a native app installation failure? (Select TWO)

options.text: Review the error output from the installation to identify the failing SQL statement in setup.sql
Run setup.sql statements manually in a development environment to isolate the failing step
Bypass setup.sql by using a pre-built image from the Marketplace debug console
Grant ACCOUNTADMIN to the app to resolve all permission-related install failures

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Snowflake's installation error output identifies which statement in setup.sql failed and why, providing the starting point for diagnosis.

**B** is correct because running setup.sql statements individually in a development environment allows the developer to isolate the specific statement causing the failure and test fixes interactively.

**C** is incorrect because there is no Marketplace debug console that bypasses setup.sql.

**D** is incorrect because granting ACCOUNTADMIN is a severe security anti-pattern; permission issues should be resolved by properly defining privileges.

[Install and test an app locally](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/installing-testing-application)

questions.stem: Which TWO statements are true about where a native app runs after a consumer installs it? (Select TWO)

options.text: The app's objects and logic reside in the consumer's Snowflake account
Consumer data processed by the app stays within the consumer account boundary
The app is executed on the provider's Snowflake servers
The app runs on a shared compute plane managed by Snowflake Marketplace

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because after installation, the app's schemas, procedures, and other objects are created inside the consumer's Snowflake account.

**B** is correct because since the app runs in the consumer account, any consumer data it processes never has to leave the consumer's account boundary.

**C** is incorrect because the app runs in the consumer account, not on the provider's servers.

**D** is incorrect because there is no shared Marketplace compute plane; each consumer account provides its own compute resources.

[Native App Runtime](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/native-apps-about)

questions.stem: Which TWO project files are required components of a Snowflake native app? (Select TWO)

options.text: manifest.yml — declares app metadata and the setup script location
setup.sql — executed during installation and upgrade to create app objects
warehouse.conf — defines compute resources for the app
network_policy.ini — restricts IP access to the app

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because manifest.yml is a required file that Snowflake reads to understand the app's structure, including the setup script path, version, and requested privileges.

**B** is correct because setup.sql is the required installation and upgrade script that Snowflake executes when consumers install or upgrade the app.

**C** is incorrect because warehouse.conf is not a Snowflake native app file format; warehouses are created via SQL DDL.

**D** is incorrect because network_policy.ini is not a Snowflake file format; network policies are created via SQL.

[Snowflake Native App manifest reference](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/manifest-reference)

questions.stem: Which file in a native app project declares application metadata, artifact locations, and the path to the setup script?

options.text: manifest.yml
resource_monitor.sql
warehouse.conf
network_policy.ini

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because manifest.yml is the required configuration file for a native app. It declares the app's metadata, the location of setup.sql, default privileges, and references the app will request.

**B** is incorrect because resource_monitor.sql is not a native app project file; resource monitors are account-level objects created via SQL.

**C** is incorrect because warehouse.conf is not a Snowflake native app file format.

**D** is incorrect because network_policy.ini is not a Snowflake file format; network policies are created via SQL DDL.

[Snowflake Native App manifest reference](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/manifest-reference)

questions.stem: What is the primary purpose of an application role in a native app?

options.text: Replacing all account-level roles in the consumer account
Controlling virtual warehouse sizing for the app
Storing Streamlit cache data on behalf of users
Exposing app-specific privileges that consumers can grant to their users

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because application roles are scoped to the installed app, not the entire consumer account.

**B** is incorrect because application roles are about access control, not compute resource sizing.

**C** is incorrect because application roles are access control objects, not data storage mechanisms.

**D** is correct because application roles define the functional access tiers within the app (e.g., ADMIN, VIEWER) that consumers can grant to their users, enabling least-privilege access to app features.

[Configure the privileges required by an app](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/requesting-auto-privs)

questions.stem: Why should a developer use `st.cache_data` carefully in a Streamlit app embedded in a native app?

options.text: It improves responsiveness by caching results, but stale data may be served if the underlying table changes
It publishes the app to a paid listing automatically
It defines application roles for consumers
It grants database-level privileges to users who trigger the cache

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because `st.cache_data` caches Python function return values and has no relationship to database privileges.

**B** is correct because `st.cache_data` speeds up the app by reusing previous query results, but developers must set appropriate TTLs or manual cache invalidation to avoid serving stale data when the underlying Snowflake tables are updated.

**C** is incorrect because caching has no relationship to Marketplace listings.

**D** is incorrect because application roles are defined in setup.sql and manifest.yml, not through Streamlit caching.

[Streamlit Caching](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/streamlit/about-streamlit)

questions.stem: What operational overhead does Streamlit in Snowflake eliminate compared with self-hosting a separate web application tier?

options.text: Reduces micro-partition count for all tables in the account
Eliminates the need for any SQL queries in the app
Eliminates the need to manage external servers, containers, or hosting infrastructure for the UI
Removes the need for permissions to be defined in the app

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because Streamlit has no impact on micro-partition count.

**B** is incorrect because Streamlit apps typically use SQL to query Snowflake data.

**C** is correct because hosting Streamlit inside Snowflake means developers do not need to provision, maintain, or secure external web servers, container orchestration, or load balancers for the application UI layer.

**D** is incorrect because permissions are still required and must be properly designed; hosting in Snowflake does not eliminate the need for access control.

[Streamlit in Snowflake Overview](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/streamlit/about-streamlit)

questions.stem: Where can providers list native apps so that Snowflake customers can discover and install them?

options.text: The result cache browser in Snowsight
The query profile viewer
The user stage of the provider account
Snowflake Marketplace

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Snowflake Marketplace is the official distribution channel where providers list their native apps, data products, and services, making them discoverable and installable by eligible Snowflake customers.

**B** is incorrect because the result cache browser is a Snowsight interface for viewing cached query results, not a distribution channel.

**C** is incorrect because the query profile viewer is a performance analysis tool for examining query execution plans.

**D** is incorrect because user stages are private storage areas in individual Snowflake accounts, not a discovery platform.

[Snowflake Marketplace](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-marketplace)

questions.stem: Which TWO types of monetization models are available for native apps on the Snowflake Marketplace? (Select TWO)

options.text: Usage-based pricing — fees based on measured app consumption
Subscription pricing — recurring flat fees for access to the app
Role hierarchy pricing — fees scale with the number of consumer account roles
Micro-partition pricing — fees based on the number of consumer table partitions

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because usage-based pricing aligns fees to measurable consumption signals such as queries processed, rows analyzed, or API calls made.

**B** is correct because subscription pricing provides consumers with app access for a recurring fee (monthly, annual), regardless of usage volume.

**C** is incorrect because role hierarchy pricing is not a Snowflake Marketplace pricing model.

**D** is incorrect because micro-partition count is an internal Snowflake storage metric, not a basis for Marketplace pricing.

[Marketplace Monetization Models](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-marketplace)

questions.stem: What purpose does of an application role in a native app?

options.text: Replacing all account-level roles in the consumer account
Controlling virtual warehouse sizing for the app
Storing Streamlit cache data on behalf of users
Exposing app-specific privileges that consumers can grant to their users

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because application roles are scoped to the installed app, not the entire consumer account.

**B** is incorrect because application roles are about access control, not compute resource sizing.

**C** is incorrect because application roles are access control objects, not data storage mechanisms.

**D** is correct because application roles define the functional access tiers within the app (e.g., ADMIN, VIEWER) that consumers can grant to their users, enabling least-privilege access to app features.

[Configure the privileges required by an app](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/requesting-auto-privs)

questions.stem: If setup.sql fails during native app installation, what should the developer inspect first?

options.text: The Marketplace pricing page for the listing
Error messages and the SQL object dependency ordering in setup.sql
Warehouse logo configuration
The result cache TTL settings

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because the Marketplace pricing page is irrelevant to setup.sql execution failures.

**B** is correct because installation failures in setup.sql most commonly stem from SQL errors or incorrect dependency ordering. The error output in the installation log identifies the failing statement.

**C** is incorrect because warehouse logos are a display preference unrelated to setup.sql errors.

**D** is incorrect because result cache TTL is a query performance setting unrelated to setup.sql failures.

[Install and test an app locally](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/installing-testing-application)

questions.stem: Why are references important in the Snowflake Native App Framework?

options.text: They replace application roles entirely within the installed app
They automatically publish the app listing to the Marketplace
They let consumers grant the app controlled access to consumer-side objects
They eliminate the need for a setup.sql script

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because references and application roles are separate concepts; references do not replace roles.

**B** is incorrect because Marketplace listing is a separate workflow unrelated to references.

**C** is correct because references are the approved mechanism by which a native app requests and consumers grant access to consumer-owned objects such as tables, views, or warehouses, without the provider ever seeing the underlying data directly.

**D** is incorrect because setup.sql is still required; references do not eliminate it.

[Request references and object-level privileges from consumers](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/requesting-refs)

questions.stem: Which TWO statements correctly describe the Snowflake Native App Framework? (Select TWO)

options.text: It allows providers to build and distribute apps that run inside consumer Snowflake accounts
It uses an application package as the provider-side container for versioned artifacts
It replaces virtual warehouses with serverless compute for all app workloads
It requires consumer data to be copied to the provider account before processing

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the Native App Framework's core purpose is enabling providers to package and distribute apps that run inside consumer Snowflake accounts.

**B** is correct because the application package is the foundational provider-side object that holds versioned code, stages, and metadata for the app before release.

**C** is incorrect because the Native App Framework does not replace virtual warehouses; apps still use consumer-provisioned warehouses for compute.

**D** is incorrect because consumer data never needs to move to the provider; the app runs in the consumer account and accesses consumer data locally.

[Native App Framework Overview](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/native-apps-about)

questions.stem: Which technology is most commonly embedded as the interactive UI layer for a Snowflake native app?

options.text: Snowpipe Streaming
Tri-Secret Secure encryption
Streamlit
ODBC DSN configuration files

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because Snowpipe Streaming is a data ingestion feature, not a UI technology.

**B** is incorrect because Tri-Secret Secure is a data encryption option, not a UI framework.

**C** is correct because Streamlit in Snowflake is the primary and most commonly used technology for building interactive web UIs inside native apps, allowing Python-based dashboards and data applications.

**D** is incorrect because ODBC DSN files are connector configuration files for SQL clients, not UI frameworks.

[Streamlit in Native Apps](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/adding-streamlit)

questions.stem: Why are references important in the Snowflake Native App Framework?

options.text: They automatically publish the app listing to the Marketplace
They let consumers grant the app controlled access to consumer-side objects
They eliminate the need for a setup.sql script
They replace application roles entirely within the installed app

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because references and application roles are separate concepts; references do not replace roles.

**B** is incorrect because Marketplace listing is a separate workflow unrelated to references.

**C** is correct because references are the approved mechanism by which a native app requests and consumers grant access to consumer-owned objects such as tables, views, or warehouses, without the provider ever seeing the underlying data directly.

**D** is incorrect because setup.sql is still required; references do not eliminate it.

[Request references and object-level privileges from consumers](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/requesting-refs)

questions.stem: Which TWO object types can a native app create in the consumer account through setup.sql? (Select TWO)

options.text: Stored procedures and UDFs registered under the app
New Snowflake user accounts for end users
Account-level network policies affecting all connections
Schemas and tables within the app's database

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because setup.sql can create schemas and tables within the native app's installed database object in the consumer account.

**B** is correct because setup.sql can define stored procedures and UDFs scoped to the app, providing the business logic and data processing capabilities.

**C** is incorrect because native apps cannot create Snowflake user accounts; user management is the consumer account admin's responsibility.

**D** is incorrect because native apps cannot create account-level network policies or other account-wide security configurations.

[Create the setup script](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/creating-setup-script)

questions.stem: What is the primary purpose of the Snowflake Native App Framework?

options.text: To package and distribute applications that run inside consumer Snowflake accounts
To replace all virtual warehouses with serverless compute
To enforce account-level password rotation policies
To continuously load CSV files from external stages

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the Native App Framework is specifically designed for building, packaging, and distributing applications that consumers install in their own Snowflake accounts. Providers publish the app; consumers run it inside their own account boundary.

**B** is incorrect because the Native App Framework has nothing to do with replacing virtual warehouses.

**C** is incorrect because password rotation is a security feature unrelated to the Native App Framework.

**D** is incorrect because continuous data loading is the purpose of Snowpipe, not the Native App Framework.

[Native App Framework Overview](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/native-apps-about)

questions.stem: Why is idempotent setup logic important in native app installation and upgrade scripts?

options.text: It increases warehouse concurrency limits automatically
It makes installs and upgrades safer when scripts are retried after partial failures
It removes the need for consumer-granted references
It guarantees paid subscription renewal

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because idempotent logic has no effect on warehouse concurrency settings.

**B** is correct because idempotent scripts (using `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS`, `CREATE OR REPLACE`, etc.) produce the same end state regardless of how many times they run. This is critical when installs or upgrades fail partway through and must be retried.

**C** is incorrect because references are still required for consumer object access.

**D** is incorrect because paid subscriptions are a Marketplace commercial concern unrelated to script idempotency.

[Create the setup script](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/creating-setup-script)

questions.stem: Which TWO distribution options are available to providers when sharing a native app with consumers? (Select TWO)

options.text: Public Marketplace listing — discoverable by any eligible Snowflake customer
Private listing — shared directly with specific consumer accounts
Reader account distribution — pushes the app to reader accounts
Stage export — consumers download a ZIP file from an external URL

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because providers can publish apps to the public Snowflake Marketplace where any eligible Snowflake customer can discover and install them.

**B** is correct because providers can also create private listings targeted at specific Snowflake account identifiers, useful for enterprise or partner distribution.

**C** is incorrect because reader accounts are for non-Snowflake users accessing shared data; native apps require consumers to have their own Snowflake accounts.

**D** is incorrect because native apps are not distributed as downloadable ZIP files.

[Listing Types](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-marketplace)

questions.stem: Which Snowflake capability is most directly used when a Streamlit app needs Python-based tabular data processing alongside SQL queries?

options.text: Snowpark
Network policies
Reader accounts
Search optimization service

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Snowpark provides Python (and Java/Scala) DataFrame APIs and stored procedure capabilities that pair naturally with Streamlit apps, enabling complex data transformations and ML inference to run server-side within Snowflake.

**B** is incorrect because network policies restrict IP-based connectivity to Snowflake accounts and are not related to tabular data processing.

**C** is incorrect because reader accounts are for sharing data with consumers who lack Snowflake accounts, not for data processing in apps.

**D** is incorrect because the search optimization service improves point-lookup query performance but is not a Python data processing capability.

[Snowpark for Python](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/snowpark/python/index)

questions.stem: Which TWO are benefits of hosting a Streamlit app inside Snowflake rather than on an external web server? (Select TWO)

options.text: No external servers or containers are required for the UI tier
Streamlit apps can query Snowflake data within the same security boundary without data egress
Streamlit in Snowflake eliminates the need for any SQL queries
All Snowflake accounts get the Streamlit app pre-installed automatically

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Snowflake hosts and manages the Streamlit application server, removing the need for external infrastructure such as EC2 instances, containers, or load balancers.

**B** is correct because a Streamlit app running inside Snowflake queries data within the same account boundary, so no data needs to be transferred outside Snowflake, reducing egress costs and latency.

**C** is incorrect because Streamlit apps typically use SQL heavily to query and display Snowflake data.

**D** is incorrect because Streamlit apps must be explicitly created and shared; they are not automatically installed in consumer accounts.

[Streamlit in Snowflake](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/streamlit/about-streamlit)

questions.stem: Why should a native app developer test in a separate consumer-style Snowflake environment rather than the provider development environment?

options.text: To make warehouses free in the development environment
To bypass the application packaging process during testing
To validate installation, permission grants, and upgrade behavior from the consumer's perspective
To disable setup.sql execution during testing

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because testing environments are not free; costs are incurred in any Snowflake account.

**B** is incorrect because packaging must still be completed before the app can be installed in the test environment.

**C** is correct because a consumer-style test environment forces the developer to experience the app the same way a consumer would: going through the full install flow, granting references, using application roles, and triggering upgrade scripts.

**D** is incorrect because setup.sql should still execute in the test environment; skipping it would invalidate the test.

[Install and test an app locally](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/installing-testing-application)

questions.stem: Which monetization model charges consumers based on how much they actually use the native app?

options.text: Micro-partition count pricing
Role hierarchy flat pricing
Usage-based pricing
Mandatory ACCOUNTADMIN pricing tier

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because there is no Snowflake pricing model based on ACCOUNTADMIN role usage.

**B** is incorrect because micro-partition count is an internal Snowflake storage concept, not a Marketplace pricing model.

**C** is incorrect because role hierarchy is an access control concept, not a commercial pricing model.

**D** is correct because usage-based pricing aligns fees to measured app consumption metrics such as the number of queries processed, rows analyzed, or API calls made through the app.

[Marketplace Monetization](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-marketplace)

questions.stem: Which tooling is most commonly used during iterative native app development for packaging and testing workflows?

options.text: Snowflake Fail-Safe recovery
Reader accounts only
Micro-partition clustering keys
Snowflake CLI (snow)

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because Fail-Safe is a Snowflake storage recovery feature for tables, not a development tool.

**B** is incorrect because reader accounts are for data sharing with non-Snowflake users, not for app development workflows.

**C** is incorrect because clustering keys are a table optimization feature unrelated to native app development.

**D** is correct because the Snowflake CLI (snow) provides native commands for native app development: `snow app run` deploys the app from source to a dev environment, `snow app teardown` removes it, and the CLI handles artifact upload and version management automatically.

[Snowflake CLI for Native Apps](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/snowflake-cli/native-apps/overview)

questions.stem: Which TWO Snowflake capabilities are commonly combined with Streamlit when building a native app that performs Python data processing? (Select TWO)

options.text: Snowpark — for Python DataFrame operations and stored procedures
Snowflake Cortex — for AI/ML inference functions callable from Python
Fail-Safe — for recovering accidentally deleted app data
Tri-Secret Secure — for managing Streamlit session encryption keys

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Snowpark provides the Python DataFrame API and stored procedure support that Streamlit apps use to perform complex transformations server-side within Snowflake.

**B** is correct because Snowflake Cortex provides built-in AI functions (sentiment analysis, summarization, embeddings) that can be called from Python or SQL within a Streamlit native app to add ML capabilities without external services.

**C** is incorrect because Fail-Safe is a background data recovery feature for tables, not something developers integrate with Streamlit.

**D** is incorrect because Tri-Secret Secure is a data encryption option for at-rest data, not a Streamlit session feature.

[Building Apps with Snowpark and Streamlit](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/adding-streamlit)

questions.stem: A Streamlit native app needs governed access to consumer data. What is the recommended approach?

options.text: Upload all consumer data to the provider account first for processing
Grant ACCOUNTADMIN to all end users of the app
Use granted references so consumers approve object access, and expose features through application roles
Disable consumer masking policies before running queries

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because uploading consumer data to the provider account defeats the consumer-account isolation model and may violate data governance requirements.

**B** is incorrect because granting ACCOUNTADMIN to end users is a severe security anti-pattern that violates least-privilege principles.

**C** is correct because references let consumers explicitly approve what objects the app can access, and application roles ensure end users only access the features appropriate to their role, maintaining least-privilege access.

**D** is incorrect because disabling masking policies would circumvent consumer data governance controls.

[Request references and object-level privileges from consumers](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/requesting-refs)

questions.stem: Which statement most accurately reflects provider visibility into consumer data within the native app model?

options.text: Providers automatically inherit SYSADMIN in the consumer account
Providers should assume they cannot access consumer data beyond what is explicitly granted through references
Marketplace analytics expose every row of consumer data to the provider
App installs automatically disable consumer masking policies

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because providers inherit no account roles in the consumer account through app installation.

**B** is correct because the native app model is designed so providers have no visibility into consumer data beyond what the consumer explicitly grants through references. Providers should design their apps with this boundary in mind.

**C** is incorrect because Marketplace analytics provide providers with aggregate usage telemetry, not access to consumer data rows.

**D** is incorrect because app installation does not affect masking policies; consumer governance controls remain fully in effect.

[Consumer Data Isolation](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/native-apps-about)

questions.stem: Which TWO Marketplace features help providers track how consumers are using their native app? (Select TWO)

options.text: Marketplace analytics — provides aggregate install and usage event data to providers
Usage tracking tables within the app — custom telemetry the provider writes to app-owned tables
Direct SELECT access to all consumer databases granted automatically to providers
Snowflake audit logs from consumer accounts shared automatically with providers

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Snowflake Marketplace analytics provides providers with aggregate telemetry about listing views, installs, and basic usage signals without exposing consumer data.

**B** is correct because providers can design the app to log usage events to app-owned tables within the consumer account, which can be shared back to the provider through a data share, providing richer usage insights.

**C** is incorrect because providers are never granted direct database access in consumer accounts.

**D** is incorrect because consumer audit logs are not automatically shared with providers; they remain under the consumer's control.

[Usage Tracking for Native Apps](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-marketplace)

questions.stem: Which TWO Snowflake CLI commands support the native app development loop? (Select TWO)

options.text: snow app run — deploys the app from source to a development environment
snow app teardown — removes the installed app and application package from the development environment
snow app publish — immediately makes the app available on the public Marketplace
snow app encrypt — encrypts all stage files for the app

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because `snow app run` automates the upload of source files and installation of the app in a developer account, enabling fast iteration.

**B** is correct because `snow app teardown` removes the application object and package from the development environment, allowing a clean reinstall to test the full installation flow.

**C** is incorrect because `snow app publish` is not a standard Snowflake CLI command for direct Marketplace publishing.

**D** is incorrect because `snow app encrypt` is not a Snowflake CLI command.

[Snowflake CLI Native App Commands](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/snowflake-cli/native-apps/overview)

questions.stem: A Streamlit native app needs governed access to consumer data. What is the recommended approach?

options.text: Grant ACCOUNTADMIN to all end users of the app
Use granted references so consumers approve object access, and expose features through application roles
Disable consumer masking policies before running queries
Upload all consumer data to the provider account first for processing

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because uploading consumer data to the provider account defeats the consumer-account isolation model and may violate data governance requirements.

**B** is incorrect because granting ACCOUNTADMIN to end users is a severe security anti-pattern that violates least-privilege principles.

**C** is correct because references let consumers explicitly approve what objects the app can access, and application roles ensure end users only access the features appropriate to their role, maintaining least-privilege access.

**D** is incorrect because disabling masking policies would circumvent consumer data governance controls.

[Request references and object-level privileges from consumers](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/requesting-refs)

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about versioning in native apps are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: Providers create a version in the application package using ALTER APPLICATION PACKAGE ... ADD VERSION
A release directive controls which version consumers with specific accounts receive
Consumers choose which patch version to install from a Marketplace menu
Each new version in the application package automatically replaces the existing version for all consumers

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because providers add versions to the application package using the ALTER APPLICATION PACKAGE ... ADD VERSION command, which snapshots the current staged artifacts into a versioned, immutable release.

**B** is correct because release directives allow providers to target specific versions to specific consumer accounts or account groups, enabling staged rollouts.

**C** is incorrect because consumers do not pick individual patch versions from a menu; version selection is controlled by the provider through release directives.

**D** is incorrect because adding a new version does not automatically push it to all consumers; the release directive must be updated.

[Application Package Versioning](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/native-apps/creating-app-package)

questions.stem: What does enabling auto-fulfillment for a Marketplace listing mean for eligible consumers?

options.text: The app package no longer requires a version before publishing
All consumer data is automatically shared with the provider
Eligible consumers can install the app without needing manual provider approval for each request
No application roles are required within the app

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because versioning is still required; auto-fulfillment does not bypass packaging requirements.

**B** is incorrect because auto-fulfillment relates to install approval workflow, not data sharing.

**C** is correct because auto-fulfillment streamlines the consumer installation experience by automatically approving eligible consumers without requiring the provider to manually review each individual install request.

**D** is incorrect because application roles are still required for proper access control regardless of fulfillment mode.

[Auto-fulfillment](https://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/data-marketplace)
